title: Read a Utility Bill
summary: Read a utility bill and find the usage amount, charges, and due date.
learnerPromise: You will be able to look at a utility bill and understand what you are being charged for, how much you used, and when payment is due.
whyItMatters: Utility bills are a regular part of living independently. Understanding them helps you avoid late fees, spot unexpected charges, and notice unusual usage.
realLifeUses: Check if your electricity bill is higher than last month and figure out why
Confirm the due date so you can pay on time and avoid late fees
Compare your water usage to the same month last year
Spot a billing error and call the utility company with the right details
materialsNeeded: A recent utility bill (paper or digital)
result: You can locate the billing period, usage amount, total charges, and due date on most utility bills.
youWillLearn: Locate the billing period and service address on a bill
Find the usage section (kilowatt-hours, gallons, therms, etc.)
Identify the total amount due and the due date
Tell the difference between usage charges and fixed fees
miniChallenge: Pick up a recent utility bill and find three pieces of information: the billing period, the total amount due, and the due date.
steps: Look at the top of the bill to find the billing period and your account details
Find the section that shows your usage for this period (kWh, gallons, etc.)
Locate the total amount due and circle or note the due date
Scan for any extra charges or fees listed below the usage total
Compare this bill to the previous one to see if the amount changed
completionCriteria: You can point to the total amount due and the due date without searching for more than a few seconds
You can explain in one sentence what the usage number means for that bill type
commonProblems: Bills from different companies look very different and put information in different places
Estimated readings sometimes appear instead of actual meter readings
Autopay amounts may differ from the bill total if there are pending adjustments
tips: Most bills put the amount due and due date near the top for quick reference
Usage is usually shown as a graph or table in the middle of the bill
Keep at least two months of bills so you can compare trends
Digital bills often have the same sections even if the layout looks different
